title: Major global data breach rocks local schools and unis
full_text:
Schools, colleges and universities across Australia have been left scrambling after a major hacking scandal compromised sensitive data belonging to millions of students worldwide.
Education resource provider Instructure, which serves many Australian schools, recently fell victim to a cybersecurity breach, which saw hackers claim to have obtained the personal data of more than 200 million people worldwide.
It has left education ministers fearing for the families of students and teachers with known family and domestic violence – as well as those known to child safety authorities.
It is not clear how many Australian institutions have been affected by the scandal, but it has left education leaders on red alert.
Instructure is a multinational company which provides several resources to education institutions worldwide, including QLearn, which is the Department of Education’s online learning platform in Queensland.
In a statement released on Thursday, Queensland Education Minister John-Paul Langbroek said: “This incident has impacted thousands of educational institutions, including state schools and universities within Queensland, across Australia, and overseas, and early advice is this will impact more than 200 million people and more than 9000 institutions worldwide.
“Early advice is students and staff working or studying at Education Queensland schools since 2020 have been affected.
“Advice at this stage is names, email addresses and school locations have been compromised in the international data breach.” Mr Langbroek added that he believed there to be no evidence of passwords, dates of birth, or financial information being accessed.
He added: “School principals are in the process of contacting families and teachers to advise them of the breach. NEWSWIRE

summary: A significant data breach involving education resource provider Instructure has compromised sensitive information of over 200 million individuals globally, affecting schools, colleges, and universities in Australia. Queensland Education Minister John-Paul Langbroek confirmed that names, email addresses, and school locations of students and staff from Education Queensland schools since 2020 have been exposed. Education leaders are on high alert as institutions work to inform affected families and staff.

heading: Data Science and AI
key_points: Instructure's data breach affects over 200 million people worldwide.
Sensitive information compromised includes names, email addresses, and school locations.
Education institutions are notifying families and staff about the breach.
relevance: This incident highlights the importance of data security and risk management, which are critical considerations for actuaries and financial services professionals.
